experiments.hypothesis: BTC 4h is the best-fit primary lane for testing scale-in around value recovery when confirmation improves across bars instead of a one-shot all-in entry. The edge should come from a broad reclaim of value after a flush, with size added only if the recovery persists.
experiments.expected_effect: 0 trades on valid data (integrity_skip:zero_trades_both_samples) — Quandalf must explicitly choose iterate or abort.
experiments.lesson_extracted: This BTC value-recovery scale-in concept never produced a valid-data trade. Even with a broader reclaim entry and conditional add-on, the mechanism still required too much sequential confirmation to participate on the lane, so another rescue attempt would just cosmetically loosen a dead grammar.

experiments.hypothesis: ETH 4h is the cleaner similar-asset lane for testing trend continuation with asymmetric exit logic where trigger, risk, and exit indicators play different roles. The edge should come from broad persistence after value hold, with asymmetric management capturing quick payoffs while still allowing a smaller persistence tail.
experiments.expected_effect: 0 trades on valid data (integrity_skip:zero_trades_both_samples) — Quandalf must explicitly choose iterate or abort.
experiments.lesson_extracted: The ETH persistence continuation with asymmetric exits also hard-skipped with zero trades. Role separation improved conceptual cleanliness, but it did not solve the core problem that the entry still failed to engage often enough for honest testing.

experiments.hypothesis: TAO 4h is the structurally different lane for testing retest-and-hold continuation that de-risks by time if expansion fails to appear fast enough. The edge should come from broad retest holds that continue, while time-based de-risking cuts exposure when volatility does not follow through.
experiments.expected_effect: 0 trades on valid data (integrity_skip:zero_trades_both_samples) — Quandalf must explicitly choose iterate or abort.
experiments.lesson_extracted: This TAO retest-hold continuation also produced zero valid-data trades. Time-based de-risking never had a chance to matter because the retest-hold grammar itself remained structurally sparse, so it should be abandoned rather than diluted further.
